USA.label: Pre-Election
USA.summary: Voter registration opens, candidates declare their intent, and primary elections are held to select party nominees.

USA.label: Campaign Phase
USA.summary: Candidates travel, hold rallies, debate, and use media to share their platforms and win voter support.

USA.label: Voting Day
USA.duration: First Tuesday after first Monday in November
USA.summary: Registered voters cast their ballots at polling stations or via mail-in/absentee ballots. Polls are open from early morning to evening.
steps: Ballots cast in person, by mail, or early voting

USA.label: Counting & Results
USA.summary: Votes are tallied by county election officials. Media projects winners based on returns; official results certified later.

USA.label: Certification
USA.duration: Weeks to months after election day
USA.summary: State officials certify results, electors cast Electoral College votes, and Congress counts and confirms the outcome.
steps: Congress counts electoral votes in January

India.label: Announcement & Code of Conduct
India.summary: The Election Commission of India (ECI) announces the schedule, and the Model Code of Conduct comes into effect immediately.
steps: Enforcement of Model Code of Conduct

India.label: Nomination Phase
India.summary: Candidates file nomination papers, which are scrutinized by Returning Officers. Candidates may withdraw before the deadline.
steps: Scrutiny of nominations by Returning Officer

India.label: Campaign Phase
India.summary: Parties and candidates campaign across constituencies. The ECI enforces the Model Code of Conduct to ensure a level playing field.
steps: Campaign silence period 48 hours before polling

India.label: Polling Day
India.duration: Single or multiple phase polling days
India.summary: Voters cast ballots using Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs). India often conducts elections in multiple phases across different states.
steps: Ink mark applied to voter's finger

India.label: Counting & Results
India.duration: Counting day (usually 1–2 weeks after last polling phase)
India.summary: EVM votes are counted under strict supervision. The candidate with the most votes in each constituency wins (FPTP system).
steps: EVM counting begins round by round

UK.label: Dissolution & Writ
UK.summary: Parliament is dissolved and the Monarch issues writs of election to each constituency's Returning Officer, formally starting the election process.

UK.label: Candidate Nominations
UK.summary: Candidates submit nomination papers with a £500 deposit (returned if they receive 5%+ of votes) and signatures from 10 registered voters.

UK.label: Campaign Phase
UK.summary: Parties campaign across 650 constituencies. Strict spending limits apply per constituency. The BBC and other broadcasters provide regulated coverage.

UK.label: Polling Day
UK.summary: Voters attend their designated polling station and mark an X next to their chosen candidate. Polls are open 7am–10pm.
steps: Voters present poll card or ID

UK.label: Count & Declaration
UK.summary: Votes are counted at local counting centres. Returning Officers declare results constituency by constituency throughout the night.
steps: Party with most seats forms government

Germany.label: Candidate & List Nomination
Germany.summary: Parties submit both direct constituency candidates and state party lists. The dual ballot system means voters cast two votes: one for a local candidate, one for a party.
steps: State party lists submitted to Federal Returning Officer

Germany.label: Campaign Phase
Germany.summary: Parties campaign on both constituency and national levels. Public broadcasting provides equal airtime. Campaign finance is regulated and publicly disclosed.

Germany.label: Election Day
Germany.summary: Voters cast two ballots: Erststimme (first vote) for a direct constituency candidate, and Zweitstimme (second vote) for a party list. The second vote determines seat distribution.

Germany.label: Counting & Seat Allocation
Germany.summary: Votes are counted and seats allocated using the Sainte-Laguë method. Parties must clear a 5% threshold or win 3 direct seats to enter the Bundestag.

Germany.label: Coalition Formation
Germany.summary: As no single party typically wins a majority, parties negotiate coalition agreements. The Federal President nominates a Chancellor candidate who must win a Bundestag majority.

Australia.label: Dissolution & Writs
Australia.summary: The Governor-General dissolves the House of Representatives on the Prime Minister's advice and issues writs for the election. Voting is compulsory for all enrolled citizens.

Australia.label: Nominations
Australia.summary: Candidates pay a $2,000 deposit (refunded if they receive 4%+ of first preference votes) and submit nomination forms to the Australian Electoral Commission.

Australia.label: Campaign Phase
Australia.summary: Parties campaign nationally and in each electorate. The AEC runs a public information campaign about how to vote correctly, as informal (invalid) votes are a concern.

Australia.label: Election Day
Australia.summary: Voting is compulsory. Voters number all candidates in order of preference (preferential voting). Failure to vote without a valid reason incurs a fine.
steps: Sausage sizzle at many polling stations 🌭

Australia.label: Counting & Distribution
Australia.summary: First preference votes are counted first. If no candidate has a majority, preferences are distributed from the lowest-polling candidates until one candidate reaches 50%+1.
